data.title: What positions will the next Federal Reserve Chairman hold?
data.description:
Kevin Warsh, a leading candidate for the next Federal Reserve Chairman, recently submitted a 69-page financial disclosure document for his Senate hearing. It not only disclosed Warsh's vast assets, amounting to hundreds of millions of dollars, but also revealed his large and extremely cutting-edge crypto-related holdings. The investment portfolio not only covers mainstream public chains and DeFi projects, but also includes the highly experimental algorithmic stablecoin of 2018, Basis. His depth and forward-thinking involvement in the crypto space far surpasses that of any traditional Wall Street elite or Washington policymaker, and even surpasses many crypto novices.

Basis And algorithmic central bank

In the asset list on the  69  page, lang="EN-US">

Top 5%Risk Preference and Venture Capital Strategy

Judging from the disclosed holdings list,Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions.Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions.Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions. Crypto assets are often held indirectly through limited partners (LPs) of leading industry funds such as Polychain, which is a typical primary market venture capital strategy. This configuration exhibits a clear combination of characteristics: "Beta + Alpha". In terms of underlying assets, he heavily invested in high-performance public chains such as Solana and Ethereum networks such as Optimism. These types of assets are generally seen as capturing the growth in blockchain network usage. text=""> Beta Basics. At the application layer, funds flow to dYdX (decentralized derivatives), Compound (on-chain lending), and Lighter (order book). lang="EN-US"> DEX) and other infrastructures. The portfolio also includes projects with more complex mechanisms and higher risk factors: early-stage algorithmic stablecoins and projects emphasizing native yields (…). Native Yield and the points-based distribution mechanism of Layer 2 networks. These two names would seem out of place on the asset list of any traditional Wall Street professional. The portfolio also includes Polymarket, prediction markets, and decentralized social networks, projects that have long operated in a regulatory gray area: Polymarket was once... The CFTC imposed heavy penalties, and the industry continues to be scrutinized by regulators during election betting cycles; DeSo, on the other hand, attempts to shake the information distribution logic monopolized by giants from the ground up. These holdings share a common underlying understanding: Warsh views crypto projects as software protocols that optimize existing clearing and settlement systems, rather than as bargaining chips for asset speculation. This configuration logic, based on infrastructure and financial efficiency, gave him a practical understanding of the operating mechanism of decentralized networks that went far beyond the conceptual level. Beyond those encryption protocols, what firmly holds sway are the family-owned shares of established consumer giants like Estée Lauder, board seats at UPS and Coupang, and single transactions exceeding $50 million. Juggernaut Fund is a large-scale private equity macro fund. Its capital reach extends to cutting-edge fields such as SpaceX and Delphi AI. This configuration structure, spanning traditional macroeconomics, core infrastructure, and on-chain protocols, breaks away from the one-sided interpretation of cryptocurrency hype. It's closer to the underlying logic of top investors: using stable assets as a foundation and betting on the future with high-yield assets. The Dual-Track Logic of Regulation and the Liquidation Paradox The attitude towards the crypto world, Warsh, exhibits a very real double standard. He demonstrated the inclusiveness of traditional capital when facing Bitcoin, openly acknowledging that BTC is a high-quality asset for hedging against excessive fiat currency issuance. However, this inclusiveness completely disappears when faced with DeFi and privately-owned stablecoins that attempt to restructure credit or even bypass central banks to issue their own currencies. In the past, traditional Federal Reserve officials, lacking technical expertise, could only manage the market crudely through interest rate hikes and balance sheet reduction. But Warsh is different. He personally ran smart contracts, calculated protocol yields, and clearly understands where the Achilles' heel of these decentralized projects lies. This means that his future regulation will not be a haphazard, indiscriminate approach by laymen, but rather a precise, targeted crackdown. He has only one criterion for judging everything: whether this innovation threatens the sovereign status of the dollar. According to Washington's compliance requirements, once the hearing is passed, Warsh must completely liquidate all crypto assets before officially taking office. While the tokens on his account will be wiped to zero, his years of experience in the primary market will propel him to the absolute center of global financial power. In a sense, this prospective Federal Reserve Chairman's significant purchase was a high-level endorsement of the underlying technological value of Web3. The old bureaucrats who only understand traditional fiat currency models are exiting the stage. With the exposure of this asset list, the crypto industry is about to face not blind suppression, but a complete reshuffling led by truly knowledgeable top competitors.

Kevin WarshFinancial Disclosure: Core Holdings Overview (Crypto++Important Non-Blockchain Projects)

Of whichJuggernaut Fund LPis the largest holding, with each of the two positions exceeding5000$ (totaling over1billion US dollars), part of THSDFS LLCa single position of 100Ten thousand-500Ten thousand US dollars.

In addition, he also passed Abstract Holdings(DCM series), AVF/AVGFSeries,THSDFS LLCIndirectly held by many funds such as Coworker AI, Flashnet, Kinetic, Mirror、Bessemerand dozens of other projects. The combined assets of him and his wife are approximately US$131 million to US$209 million, or nearly US$69 million, according to nearly 69 pages of disclosure documents. Of the 1800 assets, the vast majority are non-blockchain traditional finance and cutting-edge technology. While crypto holdings number only around 30, they represent a significant portion of deep VC (Capital Markets) investment.

data.title: Kalshi Targets Underage Access Problem With New Parent Verification System And AI Tools For Identity Confirmation
data.description:
Minors bypassing age verification on prediction markets has prompted Kalshi to tighten its controls, with the company rolling out new tools aimed at stopping underage access and strengthening identity checks across its platform.

Parent Portal Targets ID Misuse Among Families

Kalshi co-founder and chief executive Tarek Mansour said the firm is preparing to launch a “portal for parents” designed to help detect when a child may be using a parent’s identity to access the platform.

🚨 Kalshi to Launch Parent Portal for Prediction Market Monitoring

The prediction market platform will introduce a portal allowing parents to monitor and restrict their children's accounts, according to CEO Tarek Mansour. The move aims to prevent minors from using parental…

— Crynet (@crynetio) April 15, 2026

Speaking at the Semafor World Economy conference in Washington, DC, Mansour explained the idea behind the system:

“We are launching a portal for parents to basically submit their identification, even if they don’t want to be users of Kalshi, to see if someone is using it. Because then they can see if their children are using their ID and police it.”

The system is designed to tackle cases where minors bypass the US 18+ requirement by registering with a parent’s personal details, including sensitive identity information.

Kalshi CEO Tarek Mansour says the platform is ‘launching a portal for parents’.

“We are launching a portal for parents to basically submit their identification, even if they don’t want to be users of Kalshi, to see if someone is using it. Because then they can see if their…

— Semafor (@semafor) April 15, 2026

Selfie Verification And AI Checks To Flag Fake Identities

Alongside the parent portal, Kalshi is introducing selfie-based verification during account setup, with artificial intelligence used to compare facial images against submitted ID documents.

Mansour said,

“We are also adding selfies to accounts, where you can basically look at the face of a person, and it can tell you obviously if this person is not the actual parent that’s 50 years old.”

He added that further safeguards are still being developed.

The aim is to reduce cases where accounts are opened using borrowed or stolen identity documents and ensure the person trading on the platform matches the verified user.

He added that there would also be AI tools to see if a Kalshi user’s selfie looks like the person whose ID was used to set up the account.

“We are also adding selfies to accounts, where you can basically look at the face of a person, and it can tell you obviously if this person…

— Semafor (@semafor) April 15, 2026

Family Accounts Bring Monitoring Features To Trading Activity

Kalshi is also exploring “family accounts”, allowing users to track activity across connected profiles, with a focus on encouraging accountability within households and social circles.

Mansour said,

“How do we kind of create a sort of accountability structure amongst friends and families for people to be like, ‘Hey, you might be doing a little too much here.’ We want this to be a tool for good, not a tool for excessive behaviors.”

The feature would allow shared oversight of trading behaviour, adding another layer of monitoring beyond standard account controls.

Regulatory Pressure Builds As States Challenge Prediction Markets

The move comes as prediction markets face increasing scrutiny across the United States, particularly over sports-related contracts and gambling classifications.

Kalshi recently faced a $5 million fine in Ohio, where regulators raised concerns that users under 21 could access the platform despite state-level gambling restrictions. Federal rules for prediction markets set the minimum age at 18.

Ohio put Kalshi on notice today that its “prediction markets” are unlawful gaming and proposed a $5 million fine. A federal court already agreed with our reading of the law.

I wouldn’t bet on how long Kalshi will be operating in Ohio. pic.twitter.com/Gcw3GOTTlk

— Dave Yost (@DaveYostOH) April 14, 2026

At the centre of Kalshi’s legal position is its argument that it falls under the exclusive oversight of the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), rather than state gambling regulators.

The agency has fewer than 600 staff, according to government records, and has taken a limited enforcement approach in some areas of compliance, focusing on willful breaches rather than technical violations.

Court Battles And Rival Platforms Add Pressure On Kalshi

Legal disputes continue across several states, with courts split over how prediction markets should be regulated.

A federal judge in Arizona recently blocked state authorities from enforcing gambling laws against Kalshi’s event contracts, following a similar ruling in New Jersey where a federal appellate court found that federal commodities law may override state gambling rules.

🚨BREAKING: Arizona federal court GRANTS the CFTC's motion to enjoin Arizona's criminal prosecution of Kalshi. Monday's arraignment will not go forward. https://t.co/l5WgpAQguD

— Stefan Schropp (@SPSchropp) April 10, 2026

At the same time, competition in the sector is increasing.

Binance has recently introduced prediction market features within its wallet app, while Crypto.com has partnered with High Roller Technologies to expand similar offerings, adding further pressure on Kalshi’s position in the market.

data.title: Truth Terminal: 10 Polymarkets for Understanding Crypto Sentiment
data.description:
Author: Stacy Muur Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Everyone has an opinion on the direction of the crypto market. Most opinions are worthless, and indeed, that's all they're worth.

Polymarket is completely different.

You can't just express an opinion: you have to bet. Real money, real odds, and real consequences if you're wrong. When a question (like "How much will Bitcoin go in 2026?") sees over $30 million flowing in, the market's collective answer starts to carry weight.

This isn't because market predictions are always right. It's because people can brag for free on Twitter, but they almost never lie about their money.

That's the value of Polymarket as a sentiment indicator: not in accuracy, but in honesty.

The real issue lies in the gap between the heated discussions on Twitter and the actual pricing in the market. I scoured Polymarket's crypto market and selected 10 assets that best reflect the current true sentiment of capital: covering Bitcoin, altcoins, stablecoins, and macroeconomic risks. Some have deep liquidity, some have extremely thin market capitalization, but both convey information. 1. What price level will Bitcoin reach in 2026?

Trading Volume: $30.7 million | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: High

Core Odds:

  • Breakout $75,000: 100%

  • Breakout $90,000: 100%

  • Breakout $100,000: 35%

  • Breakout $110,000: 24%

  • Breakout $150,000: 10%

  • Breakout $250,000: 4% $55,000: 68% $35,000: 25% A move to $100,000 is at best a 50/50 chance. Anything above $150,000 is largely priced in as noise: the "$200,000 by the end of the year" claims on Twitter lack real-world support. However, most people overlook one point: the market believes there's a 68% probability that Bitcoin will fall to $55,000 at some point. The baseline scenario isn't "only rising, never falling," but rather a volatile, upward trend. The range is large, and optimism should be restrained, not frenzied.

    2. When will Bitcoin reach a new all-time high?

    Trading Volume: $6.07 million | End Date: July 1, 2026 | Liquidity: Medium

    Core Odds:

    • Before June 30, 2026: 3%

    • Before September 30, 2026: 10%

    • Before December 31, 2026: 15%

    The probability of Bitcoin reaching a new high before the end of the year is only 15%. For a market that claims to have just started a new cycle, confidence is surprisingly low.

The short-term odds are even more telling: only 3% before June. The market believes there's no catalyst for a breakout in the short term. This directly contradicts the mainstream narrative on Twitter that "the bull market has just begun." 6 million trades mean this isn't a minority opinion: funds believe Bitcoin's new high will either be later than 2026, or it won't happen at all in that time. 3. Will Bitcoin reach $80,000 or $100,000 first?

Trading Volume: $328,000 | Status: Settled | Result: Reaching $80,000 First (Probability > 99%)

This market has settled, and the answer is obvious. The question is simple: will Bitcoin fall below $80,000 first, or surge above $100,000 first? The result is vastly different, almost a certainty that it will reach $80,000 first.

Even if the market as a whole is optimistic about Bitcoin's upward trend in 2026, funds unanimously believe that it will fall first and then rise. If you bet on it breaking $100,000 first, you are on the opposite side of the > 99% consensus. This is also consistent with the first market: a 68% probability of falling to $55,000. Upward movement is never a straight line.

4. Will the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceed $500 billion before 2027?

4. Will the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceed $500 billion before 2027?

Trading Volume: $569,000 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Low

Core Odds:

  • Yes: 17%

  • No: 83%

The current market capitalization of stablecoins is approximately $300 billion. Reaching $500 billion by the end of the year means nearly doubling in less than 9 months.

Everyone's talking about the widespread adoption of stablecoins, as if it were an inevitable trend—Fidelity launched its stablecoin, Western Union joined the competition, and Polymarket is developing its own. And this growth is indeed happening. But market acceptance of stablecoins, which peaked at 45% in January, has since plummeted to 17%. People are closely watching the actual pace of progress and adjusting their strategies accordingly. From $300 billion to $500 billion, it's not just about increasing cryptocurrency adoption. It also requires a significant increase in cryptocurrency trading volume, or a new wave of issuances that has yet to materialize. 5. 2026: Bitcoin vs. Gold vs. S&P 500 – Who Will Perform Best?

Trading Volume: $738,000 | End Date: December 31, 2026 | Liquidity: Medium

Core Odds:

  • Gold: 57% (↑32%)

  • Bitcoin: 30% (↓19%)

  • S&P 500: 14% (↓14%)

Gold is far ahead, at 57% and still climbing. Bitcoin was neck and neck with gold at the beginning of the year, but has been weakening since March. The S&P has basically dropped out of the competition.

Core Odds:

  • Gold is far ahead, at 57% and still climbing. Bitcoin was neck and neck with gold at the beginning of the year, but has been weakening since March. The S&P has basically dropped out of the competition.

This chart is worth noting: Gold surged nearly 80% from late February to early March and has maintained its lead ever since. Bitcoin briefly led in January but has never recovered. Proponents of "digital gold" must face reality: the market, with real money betting, believes physical gold will outperform Bitcoin in 2026. Tariff wars, geopolitical uncertainty, and continued central bank gold purchases make sense, but this isn't what people on Twitter want to hear. 6. Will Monero reach $1,000 in 2026? Trading Volume: $90,000 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Extremely Thin Core Odds: Yes: 13% (↓48%) No: 87% The probability was around 70% at the beginning of the year, but it plummeted afterward, and now it's only 13%. The market once thought XMR had a good chance of reaching $1,000, but now that view has completely reversed.

The trading volume of 90,000 is very thin. In this kind of market, even if the "unlikely" side has orders placed 10-20 cents lower than the current price, they may still be filled due to someone's mistake or panic selling.

But even putting aside the trading perspective, the signal is very clear. Privacy coins are hyped up on the CT forum every few months—"uncensorable currency," regulatory arbitrage, Monero is undervalued, etc. But the funds do not agree with these claims. Currently, Monero's increase is only 13%, and it is still falling back.

... 7. What price level will Hyperliquid ($HYPE) reach in 2026?

Trading Volume: $712,000 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Medium

Upside Odds:

  • Breakout $46: 91%

  • Breakout $54: 61%

  • Breakout $66: 35%

  • Breakout $80: 20%

  • Breakout $100: 15%

Downside Odds:

  • Trading Volume: $234,000 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Low

    Core Odds:

    • Chance: 32% (↓31%)

    The probability was about 65% at the beginning of the year, but has since dropped significantly to only 32%. The market was initially optimistic about Binance spot listing, but has now gradually given up on expectations.

The chart is quite telling: It peaked at nearly 70% at the end of January, remained at 50%-60% in February, and continued to decline in March and April. Market sentiment has clearly shifted. This may be due to increased competition between leading CEXs and the largest perpetual DEX, or Binance's listing strategy of not supporting direct competitors. Binance's listing is almost the single biggest catalyst for HYPE. If you hold HYPE, the low probability of a 32% chance of a listing is worth noting. 10. Will there be another crypto hack exceeding $100 million before the end of 2026? Trading Volume: $12,100 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Low Core Odds: 77% (↑4%) Currently at 77% and trending upwards. The market opened in early April at around 55% and has steadily climbed since. Currently, the market largely considers this an inevitable trend. After Bybit (which lost $1.4 billion), Wormhole, Ronin, and a string of bridging and lending protocol vulnerabilities, this is no longer prediction, but pattern recognition. The Rekt News leaderboard provides the answer, tracking every major exploit. While the $12,000 transaction volume isn't large, the direction remains clear. No one truly believes the "no." The industry is releasing code faster than it is implementing security measures, and the money knows exactly what that will ultimately lead to.

All odds are as of April 13, 2026, sourced from Polymarket. Predicting the market is not the ultimate truth; thin betting lines are easily manipulated by whales, and the settlement data source is also important.

However, when a question has more than $30 million in backing, and the answer contradicts what your timeline shows, it's worth asking who is more likely to be wrong.

data.title: Why did the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong ultimately end up in the hands of banks?
data.description:
Foreword

On April 10, 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) announced the issuance of the first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses, with two companies approved: Point Financial, a joint venture controlled by HSBC and Standard Chartered Bank.

The HKMA had previously stated that it received 36 applications for the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong, but only two were approved, resulting in an approval rate of only 5.6%. Furthermore, both approved companies are essentially note-issuing banks, making this the most stringent approval process compared to other jurisdictions worldwide that have already issued stablecoin licenses.

data.title: Goldman Sachs applies for a Bitcoin covered call options ETF; Wall Street accelerates the adoption of crypto assets.
data.description: Following Morgan Stanley and BlackRock, Goldman Sachs has also applied for a Bitcoin ETF. This week, Goldman Sachs' asset management division filed an application with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to establish a "Goldman Sachs Bitcoin Covered Call Options ETF," marking the bank's first direct foray into cryptocurrency investment. This product will collect premiums by selling options linked to Bitcoin, offering regular returns in exchange for potential profits during significant Bitcoin price increases. This design aims to attract conservative investors who are concerned about the volatility of crypto asset prices but do not want to miss out on potential returns. This product marks Goldman Sachs' asset management division's first direct involvement in crypto investment; the prospectus has not yet disclosed management fees. This application further confirms the strategic intention of Wall Street institutions to include crypto assets in their mainstream portfolios. Morgan Stanley, BlackRock, and other institutions had previously launched related products, and Goldman Sachs' entry signifies a continued acceleration of the integration of traditional financial institutions into crypto assets. Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon publicly acknowledged holding Bitcoin in February of this year. This shift in the stance of the Wall Street leader, who had long been skeptical of cryptocurrencies, is itself a market indicator. Covered call strategy: exchanging premiums for upside potential. To understand the core of this ETF, it's essential to first clarify the mechanism of "covered call" (also known as a covered call option). The basic logic of a covered call strategy is: the fund holds a certain asset (in this case, Bitcoin exposure) and simultaneously sells a call option on that asset, receiving the rights from the buyer. Selling options means that if the price of Bitcoin rises significantly and exceeds the agreed strike price, the fund must transfer the excess profit to the option buyer, thus capping its own upside potential. In compensation, the fund receives a stable premium income each period, which it distributes to ETF unit holders, forming a regular cash flow similar to "interest." In short, this is a strategy of "forgoing some gains" in exchange for "locking in regular returns," particularly suitable for investors who are not optimistic about short-term asset price surges but hope to receive continuous returns. Goldman Sachs's application for this product transplants this mature stock market tool to the Bitcoin field. The fund will sell options related to Bitcoin-linked ETFs, and the premiums collected will become the source of investors' monthly returns. Options Return ETFs: A Transplant from the Stock Market to the Crypto Market This product structure wasn't invented by Goldman Sachs; its logic and mechanism are borrowed from the options return ETF category, which has flourished in the stock market. In the US $14 trillion ETF market, options return products have experienced explosive growth since the pandemic. According to Strategas Research, this category has now accumulated over $180 billion in assets, making it the largest sub-category of derivatives ETFs. The JPMorgan covered call ETF (ticker symbol JEPI), launched in 2020, was a significant catalyst; it currently has $45 billion in assets and has spawned numerous imitators. According to Strategas data, net inflows into option return ETFs reached approximately $70 billion in 2025, more than double the previous year. Strategas' Chief ETF Strategist, Todd Sohn, stated that unlike traditional index funds, these products use an ETF structure to package multi-layered option trading into a one-stop, continuously cash-generating investment vehicle, offering higher returns with lower volatility compared to traditional dividend ETFs. Sohn added: "If price returns are under pressure, investors want to extract as much return as possible from an asset." In the crypto asset space, BlackRock submitted an application for a similar product in January of this year, while Roundhill has been operating related products since 2024. Goldman Sachs' entry further strengthens the institutional lineup in this niche market. Addressing the "zero-yield" pain point of Bitcoin, but the risks cannot be ignored. The significance of this product design is, to some extent, ironic. One of the core reasons Bitcoin has long been criticized by traditional investors is precisely because it generates no returns—no dividends, no interest, only price fluctuations. Wall Street is now using derivatives engineering to artificially generate cash flow for this "zero-yield" asset. Nate Geraci, president of NovaDius Wealth Management, likens these products to "Bitcoin with auxiliary wheels," believing they combine the advantages of low entry barriers and brand appeal. He says: "The covered call option yield strategy is a simple way to gradually get involved in Bitcoin, like Bitcoin with auxiliary wheels, but with a sense of sophistication, which aligns with Goldman Sachs' brand image." Nate Geraci added that he wouldn't be surprised if Goldman Sachs eventually launched a full spot Bitcoin ETF. Jane Edmondson of TMX VettaFi stated that Goldman Sachs' entry into the covered call options market "further strengthens the legitimacy of digital asset exposure." However, this strategy has significant limitations in its protective capabilities. Bitcoin's price has fallen by approximately 40% since hitting its all-time high last October, serving as a stark reminder of its volatility. In a market environment where assets can experience significant two-way fluctuations, the buffer provided by option premiums may not be sufficient to withstand a severe downturn. Premium gains in a bull market come at the cost of sacrificing gains, while in a bear market, they are insufficient to adequately hedge against losses.

data.title: Polymarket is not a truth machine
data.description:
Author: Vaidik Mandloi Source: thetokendispatch Translation: Plain Language Blockchain

In January 2026, an anonymous trader placed a series of bets on the cryptocurrency exchange Polymarket, wagering that Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro would be captured. The total bet was approximately $34,000. A few days later, US special forces carried out the capture operation, and the trader cashed out over $400,000 in bets. The Secretary of State later confirmed that the operation was too sensitive to require congressional notification. Consider this: the US Congress, responsible for authorizing military operations, was completely unaware. The American public was also completely oblivious. Yet, someone sitting behind the screen of a cryptocurrency betting platform possessed enough information and wagered real money. And his prediction came true.

This has become a common saying in the prediction market industry today. As Polymarket CEO Shane Copeland put it, it's known as the "truth machine."

The argument is that, because traders have a vested interest, their collective betting reflects the future direction of the world more accurately than any poll, expert, or commentator (who bear no consequences even if their predictions are wrong). You could say that Polymarket's odds are the closest thing to the truth you can find. This argument seems to be working. Prediction markets are no longer a niche corner of the internet where only a few gamblers seek thrills. A recent dataset analysis of 364 TikTok videos mentioning prediction markets found that 68% of them were unrelated to trading. People weren't gambling; they were citing odds from these platforms in political debates, just as they used to cite polls. Polymarket appeared in about 70% of these videos. A 22-year-old TikTok user even used cryptocurrency betting odds to predict the real world's direction in a political video, and a significant number of people agreed. This is incredible. Two years ago, you simply couldn't believe this was happening. But one question no one seriously considers is: are these probabilities really worth trusting? So I want to ask: how accurate are these markets really? What happens when odds start influencing the events they're supposed to predict? What will the future look like when the whole world treats betting odds as gospel? How do we score prediction markets? Before analyzing the data, we first need to understand how to measure the effectiveness of a prediction market. Because most people have never considered this question, and without it, all the hype surrounding Polymarket and Kalshi is just marketing gimmicks. One scoring method is called the Brier score. Meteorologist Glenn Brier proposed this method in 1950 to evaluate the quality of weather forecasts, as weather forecasters were (and still are) among the first professions that required serious probabilistic prediction and a livelihood based on it. The method is very simple. Let's say you predict a 90% chance of rain tomorrow, and it actually rains. That's a good prediction, and your Brier score is low. Now let's say you predict a 90% chance of rain tomorrow, but it's sunny and clear. That's a bad prediction, and your Brier score is high. A Brier score of 0 means your prediction was completely correct. 0.25 means your prediction was the same as flipping a coin. Any score above 0.25 means you might as well have guessed randomly. Why does this matter? Because when Polymarket tells you their market predicted a 60% chance of Trump winning, and he does, it sounds amazing in the headlines, but statistically, one correct prediction means almost nothing. You need to evaluate the market's complete historical record on thousands of issues. That's where the Brier score comes in. It's the only honest way to assess whether these markets are truly good at predicting election results. A website called Brier.fyi does exactly that. They analyzed over 84,000 questions across Polymarket, Kalshi, Manifold, and Metaculus platforms. Polymarket's Brier Composite Score was 0.047. This is indeed a fairly good score. Simply put, imagine a forecaster saying, "I'm 90% sure this will happen," and consistently predicting it with that accuracy. But here's the interesting part: the "truth machine" claim begins to crumble. The 0.044 score is the average of all listed items on Polymarket. And in this case, the average played a crucial role. If we break down the scores based on what people actually bet on, we find the ratings fluctuate greatly. Science and Economics? Polymarket gets an A. This market is based on CPI data, Federal Reserve interest rate decisions, and GDP data. These markets perform well because traders tend to have some financial knowledge, the data is verifiable, and there are institutional investors with real expertise in the relevant fields investing real money. Politics? B+. Decent, mainly supported by the huge presidential election market, where billions of dollars flow in. Culture and Technology? Worse. Much worse. Then there's Sports. The overall score for sports prediction markets across all platforms is only 0.325, or -D. Keep in mind, the probability of flipping a coin is 0.25. Overall, the performance of sports prediction markets is worse than simply flipping a coin to predict each question. Think about it.


The category that attracts the most casual bettors, and is also the category that Kalshi has been actively expanding (Kalshi's trading volume was once around 90% concentrated in sports betting), is the category where the market has proven unreliable.

Now let's look at the various markets, and this is where the story gets more interesting.

Polymarket once launched a market prediction about whether Bitcoin would reach $100,000 by January 2025. Bitcoin did eventually reach $100,000. The market prediction was correct, but it misjudged the probability for most of the time, remaining in a low-confidence state for months until it surged to near-certainty levels in the final stages. Its Brier score is 0.4909, or F. Remember, after 0.25 (equivalent to the probability of flipping a coin), you might as well just guess randomly. And this market's Brier score is almost twice that value. The market reaction to Kamala Harris winning the 2024 Democratic presidential nomination was even more frenzied. Ironically, she ultimately won the nomination, and the market predicted the result correctly once again. But the Brier index was only 0.9098. This number is utterly terrible, and it cannot be overstated. The market has long been so confidently wrong in its predictions that even the correct outcome couldn't salvage it. If you've been relying on this market for your decisions, you'll be misled throughout the entire campaign cycle, until the very last moment. Now let's look at the other side of the story, because this isn't a simple one. The 2024 US presidential election was a real victory for prediction markets. All major polls predicted a close race, but Polymarket predicted Trump's approval rating to be around 60%. A Vanderbilt University study used a Bayesian time series model to compare Polymarket's predicted prices with national polls in seven swing states. The results showed that Polymarket was more accurate in all aspects. So, what does this mean? Prediction markets excel at election prediction. Especially in the largest and most liquid elections, billions of dollars, tens of thousands of traders, and widespread public attention converge on the same issue, often resulting in predictions that outperform polls. However, the problem is that election predictions may only account for 2% of the trading volume listed on these platforms. Polymarket's 2024 presidential election market alone generated over $3.6 billion in trading volume, with 63,000 independent traders monthly. If you look at congressional elections, state referendums, or any issue in the cultural, technological, or sporting sphere, bid-ask spreads can soar to 20% to 100% of the midpoint. Spreads in legislative and crisis-related markets can even approach 100%. Such large spreads mean the market knows almost nothing. It's simply two people making diametrically opposed guesses on the same issue, neither with much funding. When Fate Begins to Write the Story If the accuracy issue were confined to the prediction market ecosystem, it could be controlled. Traders betting on bad markets lose money, learn from their mistakes, and systems improve over time. This is how all financial markets have operated for a century. The problem is that odds are no longer a signal within the traders' inner circle; they've become information open to everyone. Over the past 18 months, major U.S. news outlets have incorporated market prediction data into their political reporting. The Wall Street Journal signed a formal agreement with Polymarket to include Polymarket's betting data in its news coverage. CNN began displaying Kalshi's odds on screen during its election night coverage. CNBC adopted the same approach. In December 2024, even Substack announced a direct partnership with Polymarket so that newsletter writers could embed real-time market data directly into their articles. This is why the odds eventually appeared on TikTok. These numbers traveled from Polymarket to the Wall Street Journal, then to cable news, then to Twitter, and finally to TikTok. When ordinary users see these odds, they've already been disseminated through enough authoritative channels that people perceive them as fact. What people accept are numbers that have already been "whitewashed" by mainstream media. This is the root of the problem with prediction markets: once odds are disseminated as news, they begin to influence what was originally intended to be predicted. This phenomenon has a specific name; economists call it endogeneity. Simply put, the measurement result changes the object being measured. Let me give you a concrete example. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong is participating in an earnings call. He learns that Polymarket is executing a contract that stipulates whether he will mention certain phrases during the call. So, he modifies his original wording. The market should have been able to predict what he would say. However, his understanding of market dynamics changes his final statement. Now, let's extend this dynamic to the election level. In the 2024 US presidential election, a French trader using the pseudonym "Theo" (referred to by the media as the "Polymarket Whale") bet on a Trump victory and ultimately profited over $85 million. This was no lucky gambler. He commissioned a private poll independent of all publicly available national polls, which showed Trump's actual performance far exceeding what the public polls indicated. This led to his bet driving up prices on multiple platforms, subsequently being reported by the media I mentioned, including the Wall Street Journal, CNN, and political commentators across various platforms. Despite polls showing a close race, market predictions leaned towards Trump. This single narrative shaped the opinions of millions in the final weeks. Commentators debated whether "smart money" possessed information unseen in the polls. Voters accepted this narrative, and Trump ultimately won. I'm not claiming Theo changed the election results. That's far-fetched, and I can't verify it. What I really want to say is that anyone following this should be concerned: a trader with access to private polling data that others can't obtain was able to influence Polymarket's price movements, and then the Wall Street Journal and CNN packaged this price as the collective wisdom of the market and disseminated it. A good prediction market should aggregate a large amount of information from numerous participants into a clear signal. What happened in 2024 was that one person's exclusive polling data was laundered through Polymarket and re-disseminated as if it represented the consensus of thousands of traders. If a trader can do this with $85 million, think about what those who truly have money and power might do? In February 2026, Israeli authorities indicted at least two people, accusing them of using classified military intelligence to gamble on the Polymarket platform. They placed bets on contracts related to Israeli military operations before the operations were made public, with a potential profit of approximately $100,000. These individuals possessed security clearances and used information unavailable to the public for days to wager on the war. This is the first such case globally, demonstrating that prediction markets are fast enough, liquid enough, and anonymous enough to be used to monetize classified information in real time. The Maduro deal mentioned at the beginning of this article follows a similar pattern. Someone placed bets before the secret military operations took place and won over $400,000. Whoever it was, either had inside information or was one of the luckiest bettors in the history of political gambling. We'll never know. What happens when everyone believes the odds? On the Polymarket platform, the median response time for questions is four days. The average response time is 19 days, but a few long-term trading markets can inflate the average. Most questions on the platform relate to the market movements of the week. This suggests that these markets are not making any meaningful long-term predictions about the future. They are simply pricing in near-term events. Will Friday's vote pass? What will this person say tomorrow? Will Wednesday's numbers be higher or lower than expected? This information is certainly useful. But it's very different from what people mean by prediction markets being "truth machines." The term "truth machine" usually implies that the market can tell you what the world will look like six months, a year, or even five years from now. But data shows that it simply cannot. Not at all. 99% of the trading volume in prediction markets is concentrated in the last few hours before an event is settled. Funds flood in during the final hours when the outcome is almost certain. Moreover, these markets have even larger liquidity gaps. By the end of 2025, the combined weekly trading volume of the two major platforms, Polymarket and Kalshi, is projected to reach approximately $2.5 billion. Sounds impressive, right? But the daily clearing volume in the US options market alone is a staggering $760 billion. Prediction markets account for only 0.05% of that. The entire prediction market industry, regardless of the platform, contract, or category, is negligible compared to the markets institutions actually use for decision-making. Here's the thing: prediction markets are only suitable for a very specific type of problem: binary, high-profile, short-term events involving millions of dollars. But this is only a small fraction of the many problems these platforms actually offer. For the remaining 98% of problems, prices are unreliable, liquidity is virtually zero, and the results are more like Twitter polls than financial instruments. They are building the default source of probability for everything. Just as you open the Bloomberg terminal when you want to check stock prices, their vision is that you open Polymarket when you want to check probabilities. Their strategy is that once enough media outlets, newsrooms, financial analysts, and government researchers rely on this data source, regardless of its accuracy, the product will become irreplaceable. I think this will work. And I think it should worry everyone. Because the question isn't whether prediction markets are useful. The answer is yes. For elections, major economic data, and a few high-profile events, they consistently outperform other alternatives. This is a fact, and crucially so. The question is, what happens even for the 98% of questions that are completely unpredictable by the market, when the entire information ecosystem begins to treat these market outputs as truth? Economist Robin Hansen has advocated for prediction markets for decades, describing them as a system that forces people to invest in their beliefs. In his model, the final price will be the best estimate of the current truth. But this model assumes that the market is highly liquid, with diverse participants and is not easily manipulated. Our current market is dominated by a few "whales," concentrated in two areas (elections and sports), accounting for about 80% of total trading volume. The remaining 20% ​​is spread across tens of thousands of contracts, where a few thousand dollars can cause double-digit price fluctuations. These are tools for generating attention. They work when the world is watching; they fail when no one is watching. The more people believe they are tools for creating truth, the more power those who can manipulate prices wield. And those who can manipulate prices are not a group of well-informed ordinary people, but a small group of wealthy traders who control private polls and, in at least two confirmed cases, have access to classified information. The most dangerous thing about prediction markets is not that they can be wrong, but that they consistently predict precisely on key issues, thereby gaining a trust they didn't originally possess. And this trust is gradually being integrated into the world's information processing mechanisms. The Wall Street Journal published the predicted data, CNN broadcast related content, and TikTok also shared the data. Ultimately, a trader with sufficient funds will decide the meaning of this number. This is the reality of the "truth machine." A system that generates numbers, and the world decides to call them truth.

data.title: How is the bottom of a bear market formed? Where is the market now?
data.description:
Author: Murphy Source: X, @Murphychen888

In the past few cycles, observing the relationship between cost base and price behavior has been one of the best perspectives for observing BTC emerging from a bottoming structure.

The logic is that when the price enters the cost range of a certain group of BTC holders, the price movement reflects the behavior of that group. Is it "running away first" or "continuing to hold"?

If the former, the price will encounter resistance near the cost line; if the latter, the price can smoothly break through the cost resistance. If the price fluctuates around the cost line, it means that the market is repeatedly struggling and playing a game.

... Figure 1: Average Cost Line for BTC Holders of 1-3 Months Based on long-term data research experience, I believe that among various groups, the cost basis (1-3m_RP) for short-term holders holding for 1-3 months is the most effective reference (as shown by the yellow line in Figure 1). As can be seen from the figure, in all past bear market cycles, 1-3m_RP has been a key resistance level for rebounds during downtrends. This is because holders of 1-3 months are not very determined. A large portion of them did not intend to hold for the long term when they entered the market. They may have originally only wanted a quick profit, but unexpectedly got trapped and were forced to hold for 1-3 months. When prices rebound and offer an opportunity to exit their positions, they will sell without hesitation. Therefore, we see that in 2015, 2018, and 2022, every time BTC rebounded to the yellow line and encountered resistance, it would continue to pull back; then it would rebound again, encounter resistance again, and pull back again… Of course, many of these were false breakouts, indicating that when some investors started to profit, they “sold off first,” which then led more investors to follow suit, thus creating a false breakout. Ultimately, this reflects investors’ lack of confidence in the market. Figure 2: BTC Supply Held for 1-3 Months For example, looking at the changes in BTC supply held for 1-3 months in Figure 2, there is a clear downward trend after March 29th. This indicates that this portion of the supply has decreased. There are two possibilities for this decrease: 1. Because they continue to hold, they have been reclassified into a longer-term holding group. 2. Because they have sold, they have been reclassified into a shorter-term holding group. Figure 3: Supply of BTC held for 3-6 months. Looking at Figure 3 again, the supply of BTC held for 3-6 months did not increase significantly after March 29th. This proves scenario 2: BTC held for 1-3 months was sold during the rebound, and there are currently 1.09 million coins remaining. Now that the logic is explained, let's return to the present: As of April 15th, the 1-3m_RP is roughly around $75,400, and the price of BTC is also around this level. This is the second time in this downward trend that BTC has rebounded close to this resistance level. The last time was between January 13th and 19th; a slight breakout triggered selling pressure, leading to a continued pullback. Will this happen again this time? Based on past data, I think it's highly likely; after all, in the past three cycles, there has never been a case of a successful reversal on the second attempt. Of course, from a rational perspective, we cannot make "path predictions." Objectively speaking, another possibility is also possible: BTC could break through the yellow line, but then encounter a larger resistance level above, namely the STH-RP (Short-Term Holders Average Cost Line), currently around $81,000, with 2.31 million BTC (far exceeding the 1-3 million BTC holdings). If it encounters resistance here, then BTC may fluctuate around the yellow line, meaning the market needs time to digest the selling pressure and begin to carefully choose a direction. As time goes on, the yellow line will gradually begin to turn upwards, similar to the position marked by the green dotted line in Figure 1. This signifies that the market has completed its bottoming process and entered a "bear-to-bull transition period." Statistically, scenario 1 (the yellow line encountering resistance for the second time) is more likely, but scenario 2 (oscillating around the yellow line) is not entirely impossible. Therefore, we need to observe patiently, and even if there is a temporary breakout, we must determine whether it is genuine or not. In any case, the yellow line is currently trending downwards and is unlikely to suddenly turn upwards. This requires a relatively long transition process, and this process provides us with the best time to make decisions.

data.title: Cryptocurrency rally: Is fund rotation underway, and the eve of altcoin season approaching?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

In mid-April 2026, the cryptocurrency market saw a significant rebound: with Bitcoin maintaining high-level fluctuations, a large number of altcoins began to rise collectively, with many cryptocurrencies recording double-digit gains within 7 days. Discussions about whether an altcoin rally was about to begin have regained attention.

Historically, current structural changes in the market are often not short-term random fluctuations, but rather the result of the combined effects of capital behavior, market sentiment, and the macro environment. What factors are driving this round of cryptocurrency market gains? Which altcoins are the dark horses?

I. Crypto Market Recovery: BTC Fluctuates, Altcoins Strengthen Collectively

Recently, the cryptocurrency market has seen a rebound. BTC has fluctuated around $74,000 for three consecutive days, reaching $74,978.77 as of press time, a 7-day increase of 5.9%; ETH broke through the $2,300 mark, reaching $2,354.56 as of press time, a 7-day increase of 8.1%.

From historical experience, the current structural changes in the market are often not short-term random fluctuations, but rather the result of the combined effects of capital behavior, market sentiment, and the macro environment. What factors are driving this round of cryptocurrency market gains? Which altcoins are the dark horses?

I. Crypto Market Recovery: BTC Fluctuates, Altcoins Strengthen Collectively

Recently, the cryptocurrency market has seen a rebound. BTC has fluctuated around $74,000 for three consecutive days, reaching $74,978.77 as of press time, a 7-day increase of 5.9%; ETH broke through the $2,300 mark, reaching $2,354.56 as of press time, a 7-day increase of 8.1%.

Other altcoins also have their share of top performers. RAVE saw a 7-day increase of 4270.1%, trading at $13.9 as of press time; Srien saw a 7-day increase of 51%, trading at $0.8537 as of press time; DEXE... The 7-day gain was 40.7%, closing at $11.42; WLD rose 21.1%, closing at $0.3082; AAVE gained 18.5%, closing at $106.40; ENA rose 18.3%, closing at $0.1022; HYPE rose 16.5%, closing at $45.02; TON rose 15.6%, closing at $1.41; ARB rose 15.2%, closing at $0.1171; and PEPE rose 13.1%, closing at $0.054.

The aforementioned cryptocurrencies with significant price increases are in the DeFi, public chain/L2, AI, MEME, and Web3 entertainment sectors, with DeFi, AI, and infrastructure sectors being the main focus. Among them, RAVE, which saw the largest price increase, belongs to the Web3 entertainment sector and is more driven by new narratives compared to traditional DeFi and infrastructure projects.

II. Analysis of the Reasons for this Round of Price Increases

1. Easing of Geopolitical Risks

On April 15, Trump stated that the United States was "very likely" to reach an agreement with Iran in late April. In an interview, Trump said that the possibility of reaching an agreement was "very high." On April 16, Trump stated that negotiations between Israel and Lebanon would begin the following day, aiming to secure some breathing room between the two countries. According to the Financial Times, Lebanese officials revealed that a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Lebanon aimed at ending more than six weeks of fighting is expected to be announced "soon," under pressure from the US to ease tensions in the Middle East. Overall, while the situation in the Middle East has not been completely resolved, short-term risks have decreased marginally, and market risk aversion has cooled somewhat. Furthermore, some market analysts believe that although geopolitical factors such as the Middle East situation remain potential risks, the market has already partially priced them in, and their suppressive effect on risk assets has weakened in the short term. The recent rebound in the crypto market confirms the objective fact of a recovery in global risk appetite, with US stocks and technology stocks also showing an upward trend. According to Yahoo Finance data, the Nasdaq Composite Index, dominated by technology stocks, hit a record high of 24,016.02 points on Wednesday, closing up 1.59%; while the S&P 500 Index also hit a record high of 7,022.95 points after a slight increase of 0.8%. Technology stocks rose 2.08% overall that day, leading the gains among major stock indices. 2. BTC Stabilization and Fund Rotation Historically, market cycles generally involve three phases: BTC rise, ETH rise, and altcoin rise. BTC is a liquidity gateway, attracting a large influx of funds. When the price of BTC rises, it often means new funds are flowing into the crypto market. However, when BTC rises to a certain level, the decrease in volatility leads to lower short-term returns, and funds will seek alternative assets. This triggers an ETH rise. Currently, ETH has risen 8.1% in 7 days, outperforming BTC's 5.9%. In the macroeconomic environment of 2026, Ethereum is no longer just a token, but the infrastructure supporting global prediction markets (such as Polymarket), stablecoin settlement (USDC), and AI-powered digital asset virtualizations. ETH's strength reflects market confidence in the recovery of "on-chain economic activity." When Ethereum (ETH) begins to outperform Bitcoin (BTC), it typically indicates a significant rebound in investor risk appetite. Investors are no longer satisfied with Bitcoin's stability and begin seeking more volatile ecosystem assets, marking the market entering "offensive mode." Subsequently, more volatile altcoins may be favored. Meanwhile, Bitcoin's price remaining in a high range above $70,000 also provides a stable environment for altcoins. If BTC experiences sharp fluctuations, whether rising or falling, it will have a "draining effect" or "panic transmission" on altcoins, disrupting the rotation rhythm. Therefore, altcoin rallies do not occur when BTC rises, but when BTC is stable. 3. Narrative-Driven Rally The current crypto market is driven by several mainstream narratives: 1) AI Undoubtedly, AI is one of the most important themes in the global technology field today, and the crypto market is also trying to combine AI with decentralized networks. For example, projects like Bittensor have attracted a lot of attention by building decentralized machine learning networks. The Covenant-72B model has achieved a huge breakthrough: a large language model with 72 billion parameters; collaboratively trained by 70+ independent contributors; completed on general-purpose hardware in a permissionless environment. It is hailed as one of the largest decentralized LLM pre-training experiments in history. For details, please see "Covenant AI Announces Exit, TAO Plunges 17%, Bittensor Faces Crisis"

AI has a long-term growth logic, while cryptocurrencies possess decentralization and incentive mechanisms; combining the two can mutually enhance each other's narrative space. Therefore, during the rotation of funds, AI-related altcoins often become the preferred choice.

2) DeFi

The DeFi sector is characterized by a high correlation with market liquidity and real cash flow. Once market risk appetite increases, DeFi will become a key allocation direction for funds.

Lending protocols represented by Aave have seen a rebound in their capital utilization rate and total value locked (TVL), indicating that on-chain financial activity is recovering.

On April 12, the Aave DAO also approved the binding terms of founder Stani Kulechov's "Aave Must Win" framework, approving a $25 million stablecoin grant and an allocation of 75,000 AAVE tokens (worth approximately $6.8 million) to development company Aave Labs. For details, please see "What Impact Will the Approval of the Aave 'Must Win' Framework Have?"

3) Public Chains, L2

High-performance public chains such as Solana and Sui, as well as the Ethereum Layer 2 ecosystem, are continuously attracting developers and users.

High throughput, low transaction costs, and improved user experience will attract investors' attention. Tokens such as SOL and SUI were particularly eye-catching in the last round of market activity.

High throughput, low transaction costs, and improved user experience are all factors that attract investors' attention.

Tokens such as SOL and SUI attracted particular attention in the last round of market activity.

Therefore, when funds rotate back to the altcoin phase, these infrastructure projects will be favored by the market again, further driving up market prices.

III. Future Market Forecast

1. BTC

CryptoQuant believes that Bitcoin is facing "short-term selling pressure" as investors are valuing their Bitcoin holdings for sale.

Since this rally, exchange inflows have increased, surging to 11,000 Bitcoins per hour, the highest level since December. CryptoQuant states that this is a "historically reliable warning sign of near-term selling pressure, as holders transfer tokens to exchanges in preparation for potential selling at key resistance levels." Bitcoin's rally in January stalled at its then-current price, leading to a price reversal. "If selling pressure continues to increase from current levels, the same dynamics could repeat themselves." 2. ETH ETH leading this rally will directly boost Layer 2 solutions (such as Arbitrum and Optimism) and application tokens within the Ethereum ecosystem. If ETH can maintain its strength against BTC over the next seven days, other cryptocurrencies will also see a broad-based rally. As the price of ETH rises, fiat-denominated staking yields will become extremely attractive. This will encourage more ETH to be locked in staking contracts, leading to a further reduction in ETH on exchanges, creating a "supply shock," and thus driving the price into a self-fulfilling upward spiral. A break above the $2,300 to $2,400 resistance level is highly likely. Assuming geopolitical tensions continue to ease, the next core target price for ETH is $2,700. 3. Altcoins CoinMarketCap's Altcoin Quarterly Index is an important barometer of the entire digital asset market. This index specifically measures the performance of the top 100 cryptocurrencies (excluding stablecoins and wrapped tokens) relative to Bitcoin over a 90-day rolling period. Therefore, a rise in this index indicates that more mainstream altcoins are beginning to outperform the market benchmark. Recently, the index jumped from 32 to 37, which, while still far from the 75 threshold that officially marks the arrival of "altcoin season," represents a significant directional shift. Market observers are now closely watching this data for early signs of changing investor sentiment and fund rotation.

Grayscale Investments released its list of "Assets Under Consideration" for the second quarter of 2026.

This includes various altcoins across multiple sectors:

  • Infrastructure: CC, CELO, MNT, MON, TON, TRX

  • DeFi: ENA, HYPE, JUP, KMNO, SYRUP, MORPHO, PENDLE

  • AI +

    • Cryptocurrencies: ROBO, FLOCK, GRASS, KAITO, KITE, VVV, VIRTUAL, WLD Utilities and Infrastructure: 2Z, GEOD, HNT, JTO, ZRO, W These cryptocurrencies may become dark horses in the upcoming altcoin season.

data.title: Allbirds and Wall Street's never-ending AI "name-changing game"
data.description:
Author: Doraemon, Deep Tide TechFlow

Introduction: An environmentally friendly shoe company whose market value plummeted by 99% saw its stock price surge by 582% in a single day thanks to the two letters "AI". On April 15, 2026, a name almost forgotten in Silicon Valley suddenly reappeared on the US stock market's trending topics list. Allbirds, the wool sneaker brand once ubiquitous among Silicon Valley programmers, announced it was completely abandoning its shoe manufacturing business and transforming into an AI computing infrastructure company. The new name is "NewBird AI," and its business involves buying GPUs, building data centers, and leasing computing power. Following the announcement, BIRD's stock price surged from $2.49 at the previous day's close to a high of $24.31 during the day, closing at approximately $17, a single-day increase of 582%. Its market capitalization skyrocketed from $21 million to nearly $160 million. A shoe company changed its name to sell computing power, and its market capitalization increased nearly eightfold in a single day. Does this scenario seem familiar? From $4 billion to $39 million: The fall of a Silicon Valley darling. The story begins in 2015 when former New Zealand footballer Tim Brown and renewable materials expert Joey Zwillinger founded Allbirds in San Francisco. Their selling point was simple: shoes made from merino wool—comfortable, eco-friendly, and minimalist. These shoes quickly became the "uniform" of Silicon Valley's tech world; Obama wore them, Leonardo DiCaprio wore them, and venture capitalists on Sand Hill Road practically owned a pair. In November 2021, Allbirds went public on Nasdaq, its market capitalization once exceeding $4 billion. At that time, ESG was still politically correct on Wall Street, and "sustainable fashion" was the most attractive consumer narrative; investors believed this company could become the next Nike. But the bubble burst faster than expected. In the four years following its IPO, Allbirds' revenue halved from $298 million to $152 million. Competitors flocked in, customer acquisition costs soared, and physical stores closed one after another. In January 2026, the company announced the closure of all its full-price stores in the United States. On March 30, 2026, Allbirds sold its brand, intellectual property, and all footwear assets to American Exchange Group for $39 million. $39 million is less than a fraction of the company's IPO proceeds. From $4 billion to $39 million, a 99% drop, in less than five years. After selling its shoes, what's left of Allbirds? A Nasdaq shell, a stock ticker BIRD, a bunch of shareholders, and a CEO, Joe Vernachio, who needs to tell Wall Street a new story. Vernachio is a veteran of traditional retail, having worked at Nike, Patagonia, and The North Face. He joined Allbirds as COO in 2021 and became CEO in 2024. His resume contains no mention of AI, GPUs, or data centers. But that doesn't matter. In Wall Street in 2026, you don't need to understand AI; you just need to say those two letters, and people will buy. NewBird AI: $50 Million for GPUs On April 15th, Allbirds announced that it would change its name to NewBird AI, positioning itself as a "GPU-as-a-Service" and "AI-native cloud solutions provider." The company secured $50 million in convertible financing from an unnamed institutional investor. The funds will be used to purchase high-performance GPU hardware, which will then be leased to AI developers and enterprise clients on a long-term basis. The announcement's wording is very professional: "GPU procurement cycles are lengthening, North American data center vacancy rates have fallen to historic lows, and all market-wide computing power capacity to be operational by mid-2026 has been fully booked." The implication is that computing power is in high demand, and NewBird AI aims to fill this gap. This sounds reasonable, but the problem is: Allbirds has no AI technology background, no data center operation experience, no GPU supply chain relationships, and no existing clients. All it has is the shell of a publicly traded company and $50 million in new money. What does $50 million mean in the computing infrastructure industry? An Nvidia H100 GPU costs approximately $25,000 to $40,000. $50 million could at most buy 1,200 to 2,000 H100 GPUs, while Amazon AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud together control 63% of the global cloud infrastructure market. A former shoe company, with just over 1,000 GPUs, is trying to compete with these three giants? Of course, the announcement also foreshadowed this: the company plans to hold a special shareholders' meeting on May 18th to vote on the name change and strategic transformation. One proposal in particular has attracted attention: a request for shareholder approval to remove the clause in the company's articles of association stating "operating for the public good of environmental protection." From "making good shoes for the planet" to "selling computing power for AI," even environmental bylaws are being changed—the determination to transform is undeniable. "The Economics of Renaming": A Wall Street Absurdity. Allbirds is not the first company to do this, nor will it be the last. In December 2017, Long Island Iced Tea Corp., an iced tea company on Long Island, New York, announced a strategic shift to blockchain technology, changing its name to Long Blockchain Corp. On the day of the announcement, its stock price surged nearly 500%. That company's blockchain business never actually operated. Two months later, Nasdaq delisted it. Later, the SEC intervened and eventually prosecuted those involved for insider trading. This is a classic case of Wall Street's "name-changing economics": When a concept is popular enough, simply adding it to a company's name can send its stock price soaring. The magic word for 2017 was "Blockchain," and the magic word for 2026 will be "AI." The story of Allbirds even bears a striking structural resemblance to Long Blockchain: Core business failed, assets were sold off at bargain prices, the company retained its listed company shell, it changed its name to capitalize on the hottest concept, and its stock price skyrocketed. The difference is that the 2017 round was a hastily organized frenzy, while this round in 2026 has a more sophisticated financial package. Allbirds has $50 million in convertible financing as credit backing, a seemingly professional-sounding business model of "GPU-as-a-Service," and an SEC filing filled with industry jargon. The packaging is more refined, but the core remains the same: Using a popular tag to gild an empty shell. From DAT to GPU: Narrative Changes Valuation If you follow the crypto market, this tactic should be familiar. 2025 was the year of explosive growth for crypto "Digital Asset Treasury" (DAT) companies. Many small-cap listed companies with stagnant core businesses announced the inclusion of cryptocurrencies in their balance sheets, transforming themselves into "Bitcoin/Ethereum/Solana Treasury companies." As of September 2025, there were at least 200 such companies with a total market capitalization of approximately $150 billion, tripling in value within a year. The pattern was almost identical: depressed stock prices, announcements of cryptocurrency purchases, a 300% to 900% surge, followed by a large-scale issuance to raise funds and buy even more tokens, a cycle repeated endlessly. When the music stopped, the scene was ugly. In the second half of 2025, the crypto market corrected, and the stock prices of at least 15 Bitcoin treasury companies fell below the net asset value of their token holdings, with retail investors estimated to have lost $17 billion. Allbirds' NewBird AI is essentially a variant of the DAT model. Replacing "buying tokens" with "buying GPUs," and "Bitcoin treasury" with "computing power leasing," the underlying logic remains the same: a shell company with no relevant business capabilities attracts funds by riding the wave of trending narratives, then uses those funds to purchase popular assets. GPUs are physical assets; they won't plummet by 50% overnight, but they will depreciate, become obsolete, and require electricity, cooling, and maintenance—precisely areas Allbirds has never dealt with. Every wave of technological advancement breeds the same phenomenon. In 2000, they added ".com"; in 2017, they added "Blockchain"; in 2021, they claimed to be a "metaverse"; in 2025, they announced buying Bitcoin; in 2026, they announced buying GPUs. The underlying human nature has never changed: greed seeks the shortest path, and the market is always willing to pay for a catchy story. A $50 million investment in computing power is nothing compared to the tens of thousands of GPUs already possessed by players like CoreWeave and Lambda. Yet, a company selling wool shoes can generate over $130 million in market capitalization in a single day with just an announcement and a new name. This kind of thing happening in the late stages of a bull market is never a good sign. Remember the fate of Long Blockchain Corp.; NewBird AI's fate may not be entirely the same. But when a retail veteran, with a shell company that has just sold all its shoes, claims to be competing with Amazon and Microsoft for computing power, you should at least ask yourself one question: How much of this 582% increase is faith, and how much is a bubble?

data.title: Elizabeth Warren Grills Elon Musk About X Money, Raising Alarm on Stablecoins and User Risks
data.description:
Elizabeth Warren has launched one of the first direct political challenges to a Big Tech crypto-finance product, questioning Elon Musk’s X Money ahead of its expected rollout at the end of this month.

The move signals a potential turning point, as US lawmakers begin scrutinizing not just crypto firms, but private tech platforms seeking to embed financial infrastructure into social ecosystems.

Lawmakers move before launch as X Money nears debut

In a formal letter sent Tuesday, Warren demanded detailed disclosures about X Money, a payments and digital wallet feature expected to integrate directly into the X platform.

The intervention is notable not for blocking the product outright, but for its timing. By stepping in before launch, Warren is effectively setting the tone for how regulators may treat private-sector financial products built on crypto rails.

At the center of her concerns is whether X intends to issue its own dollar-pegged stablecoin under provisions in the GENIUS Act, a framework that allows private companies to issue their own stablecoin.

Warren’s letter highlights fears that X Money could blur the line between social platform and shadow banking system.

She raised concerns over how is it that the platform is able to offer yields as high as 6% on deposits—well above the current Federal Funds rate of 3.5% to 3.75%— and how such returns would be generated.

"It is unclear what risky investments, intrusive data monetization activities or gimmicks either X Money or Cross River may intend to engage in to pay that yield when the target Federal Funds rate is 3.5-3.75%."

X will soon handle your money (if you choose to).

Elon Musk says X Money will enter early public access in April.

The feature includes instant transfers, an all-black debit card, and up to 6% yield on deposits.

More broadly, the inquiry frames private stablecoin issuance as a potential systemic risk, particularly if large platforms like X begin operating at scale without traditional banking safeguards.

FDIC gap exposes core risk to users

Warren also raised another important question in the letter regarding consumer protection—specifically, whether users understand that funds held in stablecoin-based systems may not be insured.

According to Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation guidance, stablecoin deposits are not covered by federal insurance under the GENIUS Act. FDIC Chair Travis Hill said

"The GENIUS Act makes clear that payment stablecoins are not subject to deposit insurance or guaranteed by the US government."

However, users can still get access to pass-through insurance, which extends FDIC insurance to each customer of an eligible financial institution up to $250,000 in the event of a company failure.

Warren pressed Musk on whether X Money users would be clearly informed that, in the event of a platform failure, their funds could be entirely at risk.

The confrontation underscores a broader shift in Washington’s approach to crypto. While previous scrutiny focused largely on exchanges and token issuers, attention is now turning to tech giants seeking to embed financial services into massive user networks.

X Money represents a key pillar of Musk’s “everything app” vision—combining payments, social media and potentially crypto-based financial services into a single platform.

The move pushes X further into payments, banking, and crypto, as it works toward becoming an all-in-one “everything app.”

Warren’s intervention suggests that such ambitions will not go unchallenged. Instead, they may face a new wave of regulatory pressure aimed at preventing private platforms from replicating core banking functions without equivalent oversight.

A defining moment for stablecoins and platform finance

While X Money has not been halted, the letter marks an early escalation that could shape its rollout and future design.

The episode highlights a growing tension at the heart of crypto’s next phase: whether innovation led by private platforms can coexist with regulatory expectations designed for traditional finance.

As Musk pushes forward, and lawmakers push back, X Money may become a test case for how far Big Tech can go in redefining money—and how quickly regulators are willing to respond.
